summary: I am a fifth generation Iranian Baha'i, and my faith has been central to my identity despite the persecution we faced. After the Islamic Revolution, Baha'is like me were discriminated against, especially in education and employment. My involvement with the Baha'i Institute for Higher Education was a way to continue learning underground. Even in exile, my faith helped me find community and strength during difficult transitions.

The Islamic Revolution in Iran drastically changed life for Baha'is like me. Before 1979, we had some religious freedom, but after the revolution, the regime began persecuting us severely. This included barring Baha'is from higher education and targeting families like mine, with my father being jailed. The revolution marked the start of many hardships that eventually led me to leave Iran.

Because Baha'is were banned from universities in Iran, I joined the Baha'i Institute for Higher Education, an underground university. It was challenging since resources were scarce, and I had to travel 7-8 hours to the capital city to attend classes. Despite these difficulties, BIHE allowed me to continue studying engineering, which was very important to me.

Iran is where I was born and raised as a fifth generation Baha'i. Life there became increasingly difficult after the Islamic Revolution, especially for Baha'is facing discrimination and persecution. My father was imprisoned, and my family was interrogated and accused of spying for Israel. Eventually, I realized I had no future there and decided to leave despite the hardships.

After leaving Iran, I traveled to Turkey because Iran had no United Nations office for asylum matters. My time in Turkey was very hard; I was there for six months with very little money and struggled to survive after losing all my possessions. It was a difficult transition before I eventually moved to San Antonio, Texas.

I settled in San Antonio, Texas after leaving Turkey, where there is a sizeable Baha'i community, though not many Iranians. Adjusting was challenging because I felt the Baha'i community here was less serious about the faith compared to Iran. Over time, I found my place and became involved in local Baha'i activities, appreciating the diversity of backgrounds.

Catholic Charities USA sponsored my move to the United States in 2004, which was a turning point in my life. Since 2009, I have worked with them, and by 2016, I became the program director for reception employees. My work there allows me to serve refugees and immigrants, drawing on my own experiences of hardship and displacement.

Because Iran did not have a United Nations office, I had to travel to Turkey to seek asylum and support. This made my journey more complicated and dangerous, especially since I lost all my money and possessions along the way. The lack of UN presence in Iran was a significant barrier for refugees like me trying to find safety.

My family was falsely accused by Iranian authorities of spying for Israel, which added to the persecution we faced. These accusations were part of the regime's efforts to justify their harassment and imprisonment of Baha'is like my father. Despite these claims, we were innocent, and the accusations caused great distress for my family.
